articles.title: Notes on the Hantavirus Outbreak
articles.title_display: Andes Virus Outbreak: A Cautious, Data-Light Risk Estimate
articles.summary: This post argues that the Andes virus outbreak linked to a cruise ship is concerning but more likely to fizzle out than become a large global event. The author cites prior human-to-human transmission in Argentina, including the 2018–2019 Epuyén outbreak with 34 infections, 11 deaths, and an estimated R0 of 2.12 that fell below 1 after control measures. They also note that most Argentine HPS cases between 2009 and 2017 came from rodent exposure, with only 1.8% suspected human-to-human spread, and that previous export cases to Switzerland and the U.S. did not trigger wider outbreaks. The piece ends with a personal forecast: 70% probability of fewer than 300 deaths and 90% probability of fewer than 1,000 deaths, while warning that early complacency resembles the start of COVID.

articles.title: Dirtyfrag: Universal Linux LPE
articles.title_display: Dirty Frag Shows Universal Linux Root Escalation
articles.summary:
Dirty Frag is a publicly released Linux local privilege escalation report that claims root access on all major distributions by chaining two kernel bugs. The post includes a full exploit path and remediation guidance, including a temporary mitigation that disables the esp4, esp6, and rxrpc kernel modules via /etc/modprobe.d/dirtyfrag.conf and unloads them with rmmod. It links the underlying kernel changes that make the exploit possible: one netdev commit and one earlier kernel discussion thread.

The attached proof-of-concept is unusually detailed. It shows two independent exploitation routes: an AF_ALG/ESP-based path that corrupts the page cache of /usr/bin/su with a tiny embedded x86_64 ELF payload, and an rxrpc/rxkad fallback that targets /etc/passwd to create an empty root password entry. The PoC includes user namespace and network namespace setup, netlink/XFRM state manipulation, AF_ALG cipher usage, rxrpc key construction, and a user-space implementation of the fcrypt/pcbc primitive used by the kernel so the attacker can brute-force inputs offline before triggering the kernel path.

For engineers, the practical takeaway is immediate defensive impact: systems that expose the relevant modules may need emergency hardening before vendor patches land, and the post provides concrete indicators of how the exploit operates rather than just a high-level warning.

articles.title: llm-gemini 0.31
articles.title_display: llm-gemini 0.31 Adds Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite GA

articles.title: Big Words
articles.title_display: Simon Willison Adds a URL-Driven Slide Tool
articles.summary: Simon Willison added a small web page to his macOS presentations tool that turns query-string parameters into a simple slide. The page supports options like `text`, `gradient`, `size`, and live editing via a double-click or double-tap settings form, making it easy to generate slide content from a URL. He also linked the implementation as a quick pull request to the `tools` repository. This is a lightweight utility update, useful mainly for people following his vibe-coded presentation workflow rather than a broadly impactful engineering change.

articles.title: Natural Language Autoencoders: Turning Claude's Thoughts into Text
articles.title_display: Anthropic’s NLAs Turn Claude Activations Into Text
articles.summary:
Anthropic introduces Natural Language Autoencoders (NLAs), a new interpretability method that trains a model to translate hidden activations into readable text and then reconstruct those activations from the explanation. The training loop uses two model copies: an activation verbalizer that generates the text explanation and an activation reconstructor that tries to recover the original activation, with reconstruction quality used as the proxy objective. Anthropic says the resulting explanations are more informative as training progresses, but they also acknowledge that the method can hallucinate and is expensive to run because it requires reinforcement learning on two model copies and can emit hundreds of tokens per activation.

The most concrete results are in safety and auditing. On Anthropic’s blackmail simulation and other evaluations, NLA outputs exposed evaluation awareness even when Claude did not explicitly say it was being tested; they report 16% awareness signals in a destructive-coding evaluation and 26% on SWE-bench Verified, versus under 1% on opted-in real Claude.ai traffic. In a toy auditing game with a deliberately misaligned Claude Haiku 3.5 variant, auditors using NLAs uncovered the hidden motivation 12–15% of the time, compared with under 3% without NLAs. Anthropic released code and trained NLAs for several open models, plus a Neuronpedia demo, making this useful for researchers working on mechanistic interpretability, model auditing, and AI safety.

articles.title: Smoothed polygons
articles.title_display: Smoothed Polygons via Level-Set Products
articles.summary: This post shows a simple geometric trick for making smooth approximations of polygons: multiply the linear functions that define each side, then use the level sets of the product. For triangles, the product is zero exactly on the three boundary lines, and small positive values produce rounded, nearly triangular curves. The author notes the method generalizes to any polygon and can also be adapted to curved boundaries. It is a concise mathematical construction, but it is mostly an illustrative note rather than a deep technical derivation or empirical result.

articles.title: Behind the Scenes Hardening Firefox with Claude Mythos Preview
articles.title_display: Mozilla Used Claude to Harden Firefox Security
articles.summary: Mozilla reports that access to Claude Mythos Preview helped it find and fix hundreds of Firefox vulnerabilities by dramatically improving AI-assisted bug reporting. The team says the shift came from both stronger models and better harnessing techniques: steering, scaling, and stacking models to raise signal and filter out noise. Many candidate issues were rejected by Firefox’s existing defense-in-depth protections, which Mozilla cites as a reassuring sign. The post highlights concrete bug examples, including a 20-year-old XSLT issue and a 15-year-old bug in the <legend> element. Mozilla was fixing about 20–30 security bugs per month through 2025, then reached 423 fixes in April. This is a strong signal that modern LLMs can materially accelerate real-world security auditing when paired with careful workflows.

articles.title: DeepSeek 4 Flash local inference engine for Metal
articles.title_display: DeepSeek V4 Flash gets a Metal-only local engine
articles.summary:
ds4.c is a narrow, purpose-built native inference engine for DeepSeek V4 Flash on Apple Metal, not a generic GGUF runtime. The repository includes a specialized Metal graph executor, custom model loading, prompt rendering, KV-state handling, and OpenAI- and Anthropic-compatible server endpoints. The README reports single-run benchmarks on a MacBook Pro M3 Max 128 GB and a Mac Studio M3 Ultra 512 GB, with q2 quantization and `--ctx 32768`: 58.52 tok/s prefill and 26.68 tok/s generation on the M3 Max for a short prompt, versus 250.11 tok/s prefill and 21.47 tok/s generation on a 11,709-token prompt; on the M3 Ultra, 84.43/36.86 tok/s short and 468.03/27.39 tok/s long. It also claims a 1M-token context window, 2-bit routed-expert quantization, and experimental MTP speculative decoding with only a slight speedup so far.

The most technically notable part is the disk KV cache design. ds4-server keeps one live in-memory checkpoint and can persist/resume prefixes across sessions and restarts using SHA1-of-token-ID cache keys, a custom on-disk KVC/DSV4 format, and cold/continued/evict/shutdown snapshot modes. The repo also provides test vectors captured from the official DeepSeek API and compares logprobs/token bytes to catch tokenizer or attention regressions. This is practical work for engineers interested in local LLM serving, long-context inference, and Apple Silicon optimization.

articles.title: Chrome removes claim of On-device Al not sending data to Google Servers
articles.title_display: Chrome Drops On-Device AI Data-Privacy Claim
articles.summary: Chrome reportedly removed a claim that its on-device AI does not send data to Google servers. Based on the available text, this is a privacy- and disclosure-related update rather than a technical deep dive: no code changes, model details, telemetry analysis, or security evidence are provided. The main relevance is that browser AI features can create user expectations about local processing versus server-side transmission, which matters for privacy reviews, product documentation, and enterprise policy decisions. However, the source here does not include enough evidence to assess the technical reasons for the wording change or whether any actual data-flow behavior changed.

articles.title: Notes on the xAI/Anthropic data center deal
articles.title_display: Anthropic Leases xAI’s Colossus 1 Compute
articles.summary: Anthropic is reportedly leasing all available capacity of xAI’s Colossus 1 data center, a facility previously criticized for operating gas turbines without proper permits or pollution controls. The note highlights two practical concerns: the reputational risk of relying on a highly controversial data center, and the operational risk that xAI could reclaim compute under vague “harm humanity” conditions. It also mentions xAI’s separate deprecation notice for Grok 4.1 Fast and related models with only two weeks’ notice, underscoring the fragility of vendor dependencies. The main takeaway for engineers is that compute availability, contract terms, and infrastructure risk are becoming as important as model quality when choosing AI providers.

articles.title: Introducing Postgres Query Insights in ClickHouse Cloud
articles.title_display: ClickHouse Adds PostgreSQL Query Insights
articles.summary:
ClickHouse Cloud now offers Query Insights in preview for Managed Postgres, backed by the open-source pg_stat_ch extension. The product groups per-statement telemetry into an overview, a slow-pattern table, and a detail flyout so operators can rank queries by total duration, CPU, error count, max latency, or p95/p99 and quickly isolate regressions.

The article’s concrete example shows a dashboard whose p99 latency rises while p50 stays flat. Query Insights narrows the issue to a JOIN/ORDER BY query: cache hit ratio remains near 100%, WAL bytes are zero, but temp block operations are non-zero and parallel workers are not fully launched, indicating a sort spill to disk rather than an I/O or write bottleneck. The suggested remediation is standard but practical: verify with EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS), then add an index to support the filter/order, increase work_mem for the right scope, or both.

On the implementation side, ClickHouse says the backend stores millions of telemetry rows per day in ClickHouse, normalizes literals before data leaves Postgres, and keeps producer overhead around 3% with non-blocking enqueue and event dropping under pressure. The open-source extension is Apache 2.0 and emits raw events rather than aggregates. This is a useful reference for teams building low-overhead Postgres observability, especially if they want richer query diagnostics without shipping raw SQL text off-box.

articles.title: AlphaEvolve: Gemini-powered coding agent scaling impact across fields
articles.title_display: AlphaEvolve Shows Broad Gains in Science and Infrastructure
articles.summary: Google DeepMind says AlphaEvolve, its Gemini-powered coding agent, is now producing measurable gains across science, infrastructure, and commercial workloads. Reported results include a 30% reduction in variant detection errors for DeepConsensus in genomics, AC optimal power flow feasibility rising from 14% to over 88% for a trained GNN, a 5% improvement in aggregate natural-disaster prediction accuracy across 20 categories, and 10x lower-error quantum circuits for Willow experiments. It also improved Google Spanner LSM-tree compaction heuristics, reducing write amplification by 20%, and cut software storage footprint by nearly 9% via compiler optimizations. Outside Google, the post cites Klarna doubling training speed on a large transformer, FM Logistic improving routing efficiency by 10.4%, and Schrödinger reporting roughly 4x MLFF speedups. The article is broad and metrics-rich, but it is still a vendor roundup with limited methodological detail, so it reads more as impact reporting than a deep technical disclosure. For engineers, the practical signal is that algorithm-discovery agents are moving beyond demos into repeatable optimization work across ML systems, databases, hardware, and operations.

articles.title: When you upgrade your resource strings to Unicode, don’t forget to specify the L prefix
articles.title_display: Unicode resource strings need an L prefix
articles.summary: A Windows resource string was converted from a literal apostrophe to the Unicode escape `\x2019`, but it rendered as `That 19s great!` because the string was still narrow. In C/C++ resource scripts, `\x2019` is only parsed as an 8-bit escape unless the string is prefixed with `L`, so the correct form is `L"That\x2019s great!"`. The post is a small but practical reminder for teams dealing with Windows resources, code pages, and encoding drift between 1252 and UTF-8.

articles.title: RaTeX: KaTeX-compatible LaTeX rendering engine in pure Rust
articles.title_display: RaTeX Brings KaTeX-Style Math Rendering to Pure Rust
articles.summary: RaTeX is a pure Rust LaTeX math rendering engine positioned as a KaTeX-compatible alternative for native apps, servers, and WASM hosts. The project says its layout core is aligned with KaTeX via large CI golden suites and pixel-diff tests, and it exposes the same engine through native FFI and WebAssembly. It also ships SDKs or builds for iOS, Android, Flutter, React Native, npm, and server-side rasterization. Beyond ordinary math, RaTeX supports mhchem-style chemistry macros such as `\ce` and `\pu`, and the site emphasizes predictable timing and a no-GC hot path from the Rust core. The practical value is a single math layout engine that can render consistently across mobile, desktop, web, and backend environments without embedding a browser stack.

articles.title: Boris Cherny: TI-83 Plus Basic Programming Tutorial (2004)
articles.title_display: TI-83 Plus BASIC Tutorial for Beginners
articles.summary: This is a 2004 beginner tutorial for TI-83 Plus BASIC, covering core calculator-programming primitives such as Disp, Output, ClrHome, Lbl/Goto, Pause, Menu, Input, Prompt, Stop, variables, loops, strings, GETKEY, random functions, lists, matrices, and drawing commands. It includes step-by-step examples, small review exercises, and challenge prompts for making simple games and utilities on the calculator. The piece is practical as an introductory reference, but it is mainly a teaching walkthrough rather than an original technical investigation, benchmark, or production writeup. For engineers, its value is mostly historical and educational for understanding constrained-device programming patterns.

articles.title: GitHub Repo Stats
articles.title_display: GitHub Repo Stats Browser Tool
articles.summary: Simon Willison published GitHub Repo Stats, a browser-based tool that takes a GitHub repo URL or owner/repo identifier and shows repository metadata fetched directly from the GitHub REST API. It reports commits, contributors, branches, tags, releases, open pull requests, language breakdown, top contributors, latest release, license, and key dates. The key implementation detail is using the `Link` header on `?per_page=1` requests to derive total counts without paging through every item. The practical value is a fast way to inspect repository activity and maturity, especially when GitHub’s mobile layout hides the commit count.

articles.title: Chevrolet Performance eCrate package (400v/200hp)
articles.title_display: Chevrolet Launches First eCrate EV Conversion Package
articles.summary: Chevrolet Performance is publishing the first eCrate package for classic-car EV conversions: a 400V system built around a 66 kWh NMC lithium-ion battery pack and a 200 hp motor producing 266 lb-ft of torque. The kit is CARB EO B-88 and claimed 50-state street legal when installed per the executive order. Chevrolet says the current package is designed to work with GM 4-speed automatic transmissions such as the 4L60/4L65/4L70/4L75, requires an approved installer, and ships to that installer rather than directly to consumers. Charging options listed are 120V Level 1, 240V Level 2, and 480V DC fast charging, with Chevrolet providing a bundled Level 1 charger and optional Level 2 charger. The battery is specified at 947 lb and roughly 50.2 x 71.1 inches, which matters for packaging and vehicle fitment. This is mostly a product announcement, but the concrete specs make it relevant to shops and builders evaluating off-the-shelf EV conversion hardware.

articles.title: ProgramBench: Can Language Models Rebuild Programs from Scratch?
articles.title_display: ProgramBench Tests Whether LMs Can Rebuild Software From Scratch
articles.summary:
ProgramBench is a new benchmark for evaluating whether language models can rebuild entire programs from scratch using only a program and its documentation. Unlike existing code benchmarks that focus on isolated bugs or single features, this setup asks agents to reproduce the behavior of a reference executable while avoiding any prescribed implementation structure. The authors generate end-to-end behavioral tests with agent-driven fuzzing, which lets them score systems on observable program behavior rather than matching a specific code layout.

The benchmark contains 200 tasks spanning small CLI tools and larger real-world software, including FFmpeg, SQLite, and the PHP interpreter. The paper evaluates 9 language models and reports that none fully solve any task. The strongest model reaches 95% test pass rate on only 3% of tasks, and the models tend to produce monolithic single-file implementations that diverge substantially from human-written code.

For AI engineers and software teams, the key takeaway is that current models still struggle with holistic software architecture and long-horizon reconstruction, even when correctness is measured by executable behavior rather than source similarity.

articles.title: Community firmware for the Xteink X4 e-paper reader
articles.title_display: Open-Source Firmware for the Xteink X4 Reader
articles.summary:
CrossPoint Reader is a community firmware replacement for the Xteink X4 e-paper reader, built with PlatformIO for the ESP32-C3. The repository describes a practical, drop-in alternative to the official closed firmware and includes EPUB 2/3 parsing and rendering, image support, saved reading position, Wi‑Fi book upload, Wi‑Fi OTA updates, KOReader Sync integration, screen rotation, and configurable fonts/layout/display options. The project also documents multilingual EPUB support and provides install paths via a web flasher, release binaries, or `esptool.py` on the command line.

The most technically interesting part is the internals section: the firmware is aggressively cache-based to fit within the ESP32-C3’s roughly 380 KB of usable RAM, storing parsed book metadata, progress, cover art, and chapter layout data under a `.crosspoint` directory on the SD card. That design keeps the reader usable on constrained hardware, but it also introduces some caveats, such as cache entries not being automatically cleared when a book is deleted and progress resetting when a file is moved.

This is useful for embedded and firmware engineers who care about open hardware enablement, memory-constrained document rendering, and practical OTA/flash workflows, but it is still primarily a project README rather than a benchmarked technical report.

articles.title: Python 3.15.0 beta 1 is here!
articles.title_display: Python 3.15 Beta 1 Opens Feature Freeze
articles.summary:
Python 3.15.0b1 is the first beta of the 3.15 series and marks feature freeze. The release is aimed at broad community testing before final stabilization, with maintainers strongly encouraged to validate third-party packages now, report issues to the CPython tracker, and publish pre-release wheels. Python’s release team says the goal is to avoid ABI changes after beta 4 and keep code churn minimal after rc1, while recommending that production users wait until 3.15.0rc1.

The post highlights several user-visible changes: explicit lazy imports for faster startup, new built-in types such as `frozendict` and `sentinel`, a dedicated profiling package, a new sampling profiler named Tachyon, frame pointers enabled by default for better observability, UTF-8 as the default encoding, unpacking in comprehensions, package startup configuration files, typed extra items for `TypedDict`, a `TypeForm` annotation, and a new `PyBytesWriter` C API. It also calls out stable ABI work for free-threaded builds, improved error messages, and more color in the runtime. The most concrete performance note is an upgraded JIT compiler, reported to deliver an 8–9% geometric mean speedup on x86-64 Linux over the standard interpreter and 12–13% on AArch64 macOS over the tail-calling interpreter. For engineers, this is mainly a signal to start compatibility and performance testing now.

articles.title: Seth Michael Larson: Library dependency version specifiers aren't for fixing vulnerabilities
articles.title_display: Why Library Dependency Pins Shouldn’t Fix Vulnerabilities
articles.summary: This post argues that Python library version specifiers should be used for compatibility, not as a vulnerability response mechanism. Using urllib3 as the example, it explains that changing a dependency from `>=2` to `>=2.6.3` to block a vulnerable release would create massive maintenance toil if widely adopted, since popular packages like urllib3, numpy, requests, and pandas have huge downstream dependency graphs. The author contrasts libraries with applications: applications should manage exact dependency sets through lock files such as `requirements.txt` with hashes, `pylock.toml`, or `uv.lock`, while library maintainers should preserve the broadest compatible ranges. The post notes one exception: if a compatibility range itself prevents users from upgrading to a fixed release, revisiting the specifier may be appropriate. The practical takeaway is that security patching belongs primarily in application dependency management, not in library release churn.

articles.title: Maybe you shouldn't install new software for a bit
articles.title_display: Avoid New Software After Recent Linux Vulnerabilities
articles.summary: The post is a brief cautionary note urging readers to avoid installing new software for about a week, except for distro kernel patches. It cites the aftermath of copy.fail and references two newly announced issues, Copy Fail 2: Electric Boogaloo and Dirty Frag, as reasons to be wary of a potential NPM supply chain attack during this period. The piece is more of an advisory than a technical analysis, and it does not provide exploit details, impact assessment, or mitigation steps beyond temporary abstinence. Its practical value is mainly as a timely security-hygiene reminder.
